does: This paper proposes UJEM-KL, a gradient-based attack method that jailbreaks vision-language models by maximizing entropy at high-entropy decision tokens during autoregressive decoding while stabilizing low-entropy positions. The key insight is that refusal behavior concentrates at specific high-entropy tokens in the output sequence, and non-refusal tokens already have substantial probability mass among top candidates before attack. By selectively increasing entropy where models make refusal decisions and constraining entropy elsewhere to preserve output coherence, the method achieves competitive white-box attack success rates across three VLMs and two safety benchmarks, demonstrating improved cross-model transferability compared to prior gradient-based universal image jailbreaks.
connects: This work builds on recent literature showing that gradient-based universal adversarial perturbations for VLMs suffer from poor cross-model transferability, a limitation that has cast doubt on the feasibility of practical multimodal jailbreaks. The paper extends and challenges this pessimistic conclusion by operating under a strictly untargeted threat model without requiring fixed prefixes or response patterns, departing from prior work's more constrained assumptions. It connects to broader research on understanding model vulnerabilities through probing internals—specifically how attention to token-level probability distributions during decoding reveals mechanisms of safety failures—and relates to entropy-based optimization approaches in adversarial machine learning.
enables: This work opens avenues for developing more transferable adversarial attacks on multimodal systems by identifying entropy maximization as a general mechanism underlying jailbreaks. The finding that safety mechanisms concentrate at specific decision points could enable more targeted defenses that harden these vulnerable positions without global robustification. The improved cross-model transferability suggests potential for black-box or query-based attacks that leverage this insight. Future work might explore whether similar entropy-concentration patterns appear in other modalities or model architectures, whether defenses can specifically protect high-entropy decision tokens, and how this framework applies to other safety objectives beyond refusal behavior.
